plus.analyst_notes:
Closes https://github.com/vz-risk/VCDB/issues/23569
Encoded by AI — encode-veris-incident skill version 20260713T202114Z

Sources consulted: Daring Fireball (https://daringfireball.net/linked/2026/06/28/puffpal-passport-leak); MMJ Daily (https://www.mmjdaily.com/article/9848605/cannabis-club-systems-breach-exposed-985-000-member-photo-ids-at-spanish-clubs/); Boing Boing (https://boingboing.net/2026/06/28/a-million-passports-leaked-online-by-marijuana-club-portal.html).

Company structure: Cannabis Club Systems (CCS, formerly Nefos Solutions) is an Irish company that built and operated the PuffPal backend. Their outsourcing firm 9Series wrote the insecure code. CCS severed ties with 9Series after the breach was reported. The template label Error-int-misconfig-srv was applied, but sources clearly identify 9Series as a separate outsourcing company (not internal CCS staff), so actor coded as partner.

Misconfiguration: Member identity documents (passport scans, driver's licenses) were stored at predictable public URLs (https://ccsnubev2.com/v8/images/_{club}/ID/{user_id}-front.jpg) with no authentication required. Hardcoded Stripe API keys were also found in the app (programming error). Clubs uploaded approximately 5,000 new photo IDs daily to these insecure endpoints.

Timeline: Security researcher Sammy Azdoufal decompiled the PuffPal app and discovered the vulnerabilities. A re-exposure of data occurred June 9, 2026. Article published June 18, 2026 (mmjdaily) and June 28, 2026 (Daring Fireball). The researcher notified Nefos/CCS on approximately April 22, 2026 (72-hour GDPR notification window acknowledged as missed). Using June 2026 as the incident month since the original misconfiguration date when 9Series deployed the code is unknown.

Scope: 985,000 photo IDs exposed per mmjdaily. Primarily Spanish cannabis clubs; approximately 30,000 US members also included. Celebrity profiles also in the compromised dataset.

Data: Sensitive Personal for passport scans, driver's licenses, passport numbers. Personal for phone numbers, home addresses. Cannabis consumption preferences coded as Personal (health-adjacent but not clinical medical records per source description).

Victim: Cannabis Club Systems / Nefos Solutions is the primary victim as the provider whose infrastructure was misconfigured. Multiple Spanish cannabis clubs are secondary victims whose members' data was exposed; specific club names not available.

Employee count: CCS/Nefos is a small startup; coded 1 to 10.

DBIR year 2027 derived from June 2026 incident date (Jun is in Jan-Oct window → Y+1 = 2027).

event_chain.summary: Outsourcing firm 9Series misconfigured the CCS backend, storing 985,000 member photo IDs at publicly accessible unauthenticated URLs

summary: Cannabis Club Systems (CCS, formerly Nefos Solutions), an Irish company operating the PuffPal backend for Spanish cannabis clubs, exposed approximately 985,000 member identity documents including passport scans and driver's licenses due to critical security misconfigurations. Its outsourcing firm 9Series stored photo IDs at publicly accessible URLs with predictable paths and no authentication, and hardcoded Stripe API keys in the app. Security researcher Sammy Azdoufal discovered the vulnerabilities and reported them in approximately April 2026, but CCS failed to respond within GDPR's 72-hour notification window. A re-exposure occurred June 9, 2026. CCS co-founder acknowledged the breach and announced they were severing ties with 9Series.
